---
title: "Federal Spending"
description: "What the federal government raises and spends — over time, by type, by department, and by function"
---

This page looks at **federal** government spending: how it has grown, what it is
spent on, and which departments it flows through. It complements the
[Public Finances](../fiscal/index.qmd) page (which compares Canada's
*general*-government finances with OECD peers) and the
[Government Employment](index.qmd) page (the people behind the payroll line).

Most federal spending is not the government
operating itself: it is **transfers**, money moved to people (benefits like the
Canada Child Benefit and elderly benefits) and to other governments (health and
social transfers to the provinces).

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
from pipeline.config import get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import (single_line, lines_over_time, stacked_area,
                             category_bar, category_bar_views)
```

## Revenue and spending

Measured against the size of the economy, federal revenue and spending have been
relatively stable for decades — typically in the mid-to-high teens as a share of
GDP — punctuated by sharp, temporary jumps in spending during recessions, most
dramatically the COVID-19 response in 2020. The gap between the two lines is the
budget surplus (revenue above spending) or deficit (spending above revenue).

```{python}
#| echo: false
fin_path = "data/government/statcan_federal_finance.csv"
if os.path.exists(fin_path):
    fin = pd.read_csv(fin_path)
    g = fin.dropna(subset=["revenue_pct_gdp"])
    m = g.melt(id_vars="year", value_vars=["revenue_pct_gdp", "expenditure_pct_gdp"],
               var_name="metric", value_name="pct")
    m["metric"] = m["metric"].map({"revenue_pct_gdp": "Revenue",
                                   "expenditure_pct_gdp": "Total spending"})
    fig = lines_over_time(m, "year", "pct", "metric", yaxis_title="% of GDP",
        yticksuffix="%", hover_decimals=1,
        colors={"Total spending": "#d62728", "Revenue": "#4e79a7"},
        group_order=["Total spending", "Revenue"], legend_orientation="h",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 36-10-0477-01 (federal) and 36-10-0222-01 (GDP) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fin_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

In dollar terms the federal government is far larger than it was in 1961 — but so
is the economy and the population, which is why the share-of-GDP view above is the
fairer measure of size. (These are nominal dollars, not adjusted for inflation.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(fin_path):
    fin = pd.read_csv(fin_path)
    m = fin.melt(id_vars="year", value_vars=["revenue", "expenditure"],
                 var_name="metric", value_name="val")
    m["valb"] = m["val"] / 1000.0
    m["metric"] = m["metric"].map({"revenue": "Revenue", "expenditure": "Total spending"})
    fig = lines_over_time(m, "year", "valb", "metric", yaxis_title="$ billions (nominal)",
        ytickprefix="$", hover_decimals=1,
        colors={"Total spending": "#d62728", "Revenue": "#4e79a7"},
        group_order=["Total spending", "Revenue"], legend_orientation="h",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 36-10-0477-01 (federal general government, nominal dollars) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fin_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## The budget balance, by era

The gap between revenue and spending is the federal **budget balance** — a surplus when revenue exceeds spending, a deficit when it falls short. This view makes the balance explicit as a share of GDP, with the periods in office of successive federal governments shaded for reference. (Shares of GDP begin in 1981; fiscal years are matched to governments approximately.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
import plotly.graph_objects as go
if os.path.exists(fin_path):
    fin = pd.read_csv(fin_path)
    b = fin.dropna(subset=["revenue_pct_gdp", "expenditure_pct_gdp"]).copy()
    b["balance"] = b["revenue_pct_gdp"] - b["expenditure_pct_gdp"]
    b = b.sort_values("year")
    bal_colors = ["#4e79a7" if v >= 0 else "#d62728" for v in b["balance"]]
    # Pre-format the hover in Python: Quarto's bundled Plotly drops the '+' sign-format
    # in hovertemplate (see the economics quarterly-GDP chart), and the surplus/deficit
    # sign is the point here. <0.05 rounds to a clean "0.0%" (no "-0.0%").
    b["hover"] = [f"{int(yr)}: {'0.0' if abs(v) < 0.05 else format(v, '+.1f')}% of GDP"
                  for yr, v in zip(b["year"], b["balance"])]
    figb = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=b["year"], y=b["balance"], marker_color=bal_colors,
        customdata=b["hover"], hovertemplate="%{customdata}<extra></extra>"))
    # Neutral by-era bands (successive federal governments) — descriptive only.
    eras = [(1981, "Trudeau"), (1984, "Mulroney"), (1993, "Chrétien / Martin"),
            (2006, "Harper"), (2015, "Trudeau")]
    ymax_year = int(b["year"].max())
    for i, (start, name) in enumerate(eras):
        end = eras[i + 1][0] if i + 1 < len(eras) else ymax_year + 1
        if i % 2 == 0:
            figb.add_vrect(x0=start - 0.5, x1=end - 0.5, fillcolor="#000000",
                           opacity=0.04, line_width=0, layer="below")
        figb.add_annotation(x=(start - 0.5 + end - 0.5) / 2, y=1.0, yref="paper",
            yanchor="bottom", text=name, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=9, color="#888"))
    figb.add_hline(y=0, line_color="#888", line_width=1)
    figb.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, bargap=0.15, showlegend=False,
        yaxis=dict(title="Budget balance (% of GDP)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   ticksuffix="%", zeroline=False),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        margin=dict(t=50, b=90, r=20))
    figb.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada 36-10-0477-01 (federal) and 36-10-0222-01 (GDP) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fin_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.18, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figb.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## What the money is spent on — by type

The federal government's own statement of operations splits spending by economic
type. **Transfers dominate**: grants (largely to other governments and
organizations) plus social benefits (to individuals) are well over half of all
spending. The cost of the federal **workforce** — compensation of employees, shown
in red — is a comparatively modest share, smaller than the interest paid on the
public debt in recent years.

```{python}
#| echo: false
exp_path = "data/government/statcan_federal_expense_by_type.csv"
EXP_COLORS = {
    "Grants": "#f28e2b", "Social benefits": "#4e79a7",
    "Compensation of employees": "#d62728", "Interest on debt": "#59a14f",
    "Other expense": "#b07aa1", "Goods & services": "#76b7b2",
    "Capital consumption": "#edc948", "Subsidies": "#9c755f",
}
if os.path.exists(exp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(exp_path)
    d = df[df["category"].isin(EXP_COLORS)]
    fig = stacked_area(d, "year", "value_millions", "category",
        yaxis_title="$ billions", value_scale=1000.0, ytickprefix="$", hover_decimals=1,
        colors=EXP_COLORS, group_order=list(EXP_COLORS.keys()),
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 10-10-0016-01 (federal government, statement of operations) | Data as of: {get_data_date(exp_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

A more detailed breakdown, by **standard object** (the categories used in the
Public Accounts of Canada), tells the same story for the most recent year:
transfer payments are about three-fifths of spending, personnel about a seventh,
and public debt charges about a tenth.

```{python}
#| echo: false
obj_path = "data/government/infobase_federal_by_object.csv"
if os.path.exists(obj_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(obj_path)
    fig = category_bar(df, "object", "value", xaxis_title="$ billions",
        value_scale=1e9, value_fmt=",.1f", tickprefix="$",
        highlight={"Personnel"},
        source_note=f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (GC InfoBase) / Public Accounts of Canada | Data as of: {get_data_date(obj_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

## Which departments the money flows through

Ranking departments by spending gives a very different picture from ranking them
by **headcount** (on the [employment page](index.qmd)). The Department of Finance
and Employment and Social Development Canada lead by a wide margin — not because
they are large employers, but because the major transfers run through them: health
and social transfers to the provinces and public debt charges (Finance), and
elderly and family benefits (ESDC). People-heavy departments like National Defence
and the Canada Revenue Agency spend far less per employee.

Spending is also strikingly **concentrated**. The two largest departments alone
account for about **half of all federal spending**, while most departments — even
sizeable science and regulatory bodies like Environment and Climate Change Canada —
each make up **well under 1%**. Use the **dropdown** to change the view: the 25 or
50 largest departments, or thematic groups — science, research & the environment;
public safety & security; or the regional development agencies. The percentage on
each bar is that department's share of total federal spending.

```{python}
#| echo: false
depts_path = "data/government/infobase_federal_by_dept.csv"
SCIENCE = ["Fisheries and Oceans Canada", "Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada",
    "Natural Resources Canada", "Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada",
    "Environment and Climate Change Canada", "National Research Council Canada",
    "Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada", "Atomic Energy of Canada Limited",
    "Canadian Institutes of Health Research", "Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada",
    "Canadian Food Inspection Agency", "Statistics Canada", "Canadian Space Agency",
    "Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission"]
SAFETY = ["National Defence", "Royal Canadian Mounted Police", "Correctional Service Canada",
    "Canada Border Services Agency", "Public Safety Canada",
    "Communications Security Establishment Canada", "Canadian Security Intelligence Service",
    "Parole Board of Canada"]
REGIONAL = ["Atlantic Canada Opportunities Agency", "Prairies Economic Development Canada",
    "Canada Economic Development for Quebec Regions",
    "Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario",
    "Pacific Economic Development Agency of Canada", "Canadian Northern Economic Development Agency",
    "Federal Economic Development Agency for Northern Ontario"]
if os.path.exists(depts_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(depts_path)
    df["share"] = df["value"] / df["value"].sum() * 100
    # adaptive precision: 1 decimal at/above 1%, 2 decimals below so small
    # departments (e.g. the Nuclear Safety Commission, ~0.04%) don't read as 0.0%
    df["share_label"] = df["share"].map(lambda s: f"{s:.1f}%" if s >= 1 else f"{s:.2f}%")
    grp = lambda names: df[df["department"].isin(names)]
    views = [("Top 25 departments", df.nlargest(25, "value")),
             ("Top 50 departments", df.nlargest(50, "value")),
             ("Science, research & environment", grp(SCIENCE)),
             ("Public safety & security", grp(SAFETY)),
             ("Regional development agencies", grp(REGIONAL))]
    fig = category_bar_views(views, "department", "value", xaxis_title="$ billions",
        value_scale=1e9, value_fmt=",.1f", tickprefix="$", text_col="share_label", text_fmt="{}",
        source_note=f"Source: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (GC InfoBase) / Public Accounts of Canada | Data as of: {get_data_date(depts_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

## What governments spend on — by function

Finally, a functional view: what the public sector funds, classified by purpose.
Social protection (pensions, benefits), health, and education are the largest
functions — the core of the modern welfare state.

::: {.callout-important}
## This chart is all governments combined, not federal alone
A clean **federal-only** functional breakdown is not published: Statistics
Canada's functional series consolidates federal, provincial/territorial, and local
government (and the Canada and Quebec Pension Plans) together. This matters because
the federal government funds much of health and education *indirectly*, through
transfers to the provinces that deliver those services. So read the chart below as
"what Canadian governments collectively spend on," not "what Ottawa spends on."
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
fn_path = "data/government/statcan_govt_spending_by_function.csv"
if os.path.exists(fn_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(fn_path)
    latest = df[df["year"] == df["year"].max()].copy()
    latest["function"] = latest["function"].replace(
        {"Social protection": "Social protection (pensions, benefits)"})
    yr = int(df["year"].max())
    fig = category_bar(latest, "function", "value_millions", xaxis_title=f"$ billions ({yr}, all governments)",
        value_scale=1000.0, value_fmt=",.0f", tickprefix="$",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 10-10-0005-01 (CCOFOG, consolidated Canadian general government) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fn_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Run the data pipeline to generate this chart.")
```

## Download data

- [Federal revenue, spending & balance, 1961– (CSV)](../data/government/statcan_federal_finance.csv) — Statistics Canada 36-10-0477-01 / 36-10-0222-01
- [Federal expense by economic type (CSV)](../data/government/statcan_federal_expense_by_type.csv) — Statistics Canada 10-10-0016-01
- [Federal spending by standard object (CSV)](../data/government/infobase_federal_by_object.csv) — GC InfoBase / Public Accounts of Canada
- [Federal spending by department (CSV)](../data/government/infobase_federal_by_dept.csv) — GC InfoBase / Public Accounts of Canada
- [Government spending by function (CSV)](../data/government/statcan_govt_spending_by_function.csv) — Statistics Canada 10-10-0005-01 (CCOFOG)
